data.title: The Evolving Impact Of Tariffs On CFOs' Outlooks
data.description:
By Zach Edwards | Brent Meyer | Daniel Weitz

The breadth and magnitude of tariff imposition continue to evolve. Similarly, the effect of tariffs on CFOs' outlooks has changed since we first examined the topic prior to the events of early April. Concern over tariffs expressed by financial decision-makers in The CFO Survey has risen sharply during the past two quarters. In the most recent survey, which closed on June 6, 40 percent of respondents cited tariffs or trade policy as their firm's most pressing concern. Prior to mid-2024, these concerns were essentially nonexistent (see figure 1).

Figure 1: Trade/tariffs and uncertainty have become the most pressing concerns among CFOs surveyed

To understand the evolution of firms' expectations amid a fast-changing trade policy landscape, we analyze a matched sample of firms responding in each of the last three quarters (during which time tariff concerns increased dramatically) and reexamine how CFOs' expectations evolved for firms that sourced their inputs/supplies from abroad versus those that did not.

Firms exposed to tariffs see much dimmer economic prospects

Relative to firms' views at the beginning of the year, the economic outlook of most firms has deteriorated. Even so, we continue to observe a dichotomy between firms sourcing their inputs/supplies from the United States and those importing them from abroad. The latter category of firms expects a much slower growth trajectory for the overall US economy and assigns double the probability to negative real GDP growth as those not importing from abroad (see figures 2 and 3). Interestingly, the delineation appears to be centered on whether a firm sources inputs from abroad, as we do not observe any meaningful difference based on the share of inputs/supplies sourced internationally.

Firms sourcing their inputs/supplies from abroad expect slower economic growth and anticipate a higher probability of negative GDP growth than those sourcing domestically

Firms ratchet up price and cost expectations, further downgrade expected revenue

Compared to last quarter, firms importing supplies/inputs from abroad expect even worse performance than their peers across most key metrics. Figure 4 demonstrates that expected unit cost growth has increased for all importing firms, with firms more heavily reliant on imports expecting the highest unit cost increases. The expected unit costs are more than a percentage point higher than what firms expected last quarter, and in most cases they are more than double what firms expected in the fourth quarter of last year. This pattern is quite striking for firms that source more than 30 percent of their inputs from abroad. From the fourth quarter of 2024 to the second quarter of 2025, these firms have revised up their unit cost growth expectations for 2025 from 3.3 percent to 6.8 percent and have ratcheted up their anticipated price growth for 2025 from 3.4 percent to 5.6 percent. That tariff-impacted firms expect to increase prices by 70 to 80 percent of their unit cost growth suggests that margins will likely shrink as these firms absorb some portion of this cost rather than pass it through to customers.

Interestingly, figure 5 reveals that all firms—including those sourcing all supplies/inputs domestically and expecting more subdued unit cost growth—have revised higher their expected price growth from last quarter. Firms that exclusively source inputs/supplies domestically see their price growth as outpacing unit cost growth in 2025 (4.6 percent versus 4.1 percent, respectively), suggesting some growth in margins and, at least in aggregate, consistent with the notion that anticipated price growth from tariff-impacted firms will allow nonimpacted competitors to raise prices (albeit more modestly) without sacrificing market share.

Figure 4: Expected unit cost growth has increased for all importing firms, with firms more heavily reliant on imports expecting the highest unit cost increases

Figure 5: All firms—including those sourcing all supplies/inputs domestically and expecting more subdued unit cost growth—have revised higher their expected price growth from last quarter

Firms sourcing all supplies/inputs domestically expect significantly higher revenue and employment growth than their peers (see figures 6 and 7). The sizeable increase in expected employment growth among nonimporting firms might result from their much stronger revenue growth expectations, consistent with the potential for these firms to capture market share.

Firms that sourced inputs/supplies from tariff-hit countries have downgraded their expected revenue growth relative to last quarter, though their employment growth has curiously remained only little changed since the fourth quarter of last year.

Figure 6: Firms sourcing all supplies/inputs domestically expect significantly higher revenue growth than their peers

Figure 7: Firms sourcing all supplies/inputs domestically expect significantly higher employment growth than their peers

Looking across all three quarters, we observe a meaningful deterioration in firms' expected performance between the first and second quarter surveys, which corresponds to the period when most of the additional tariffs were announced and when uncertainty surrounding trade policy peaked (see figure 8). To the extent that greater certainty exists surrounding trade policy, or that progress in negotiating policies more favorable to respondents occurs, we might expect their expected performance to improve somewhat moving forward.

Figure 8: A meaningful deterioration in firms’ expected performance emerged between the Q1 and Q2 surveys, when most of the additional tariffs were announced and uncertainty surrounding trade policy peaked

Conclusion

Firms' outlooks continued to darken in the second quarter. Respondents to The CFO Survey have revised downward their expectations for real GDP growth and sales revenue growth, and at the same time they anticipate much higher cost and price growth during 2025. Tariff-exposed firms appear to be driving much of the overall deterioration in own-firm expectations. That said, at least to date, firms not importing supplies/inputs from abroad anticipate raising prices too, suggesting some ability to grow margins as their competitors face sharply higher cost growth due to tariffs. Trade policy remains fluid, and further developments are likely to have an impact on firms' expectations for the economy and their own performance. We will continue to closely monitor these developments and provide additional updates later this year.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kura Sushi: Despite Expansion, The Q3 Outlook Isn't Rosy
data.description:
Kura Sushi USA, Inc. (NASDAQ:KRUS) has continued to expand. The revolving sushi bar chain still eyes quite a weak underlying fiscal Q3 performance for the upcoming report, represented by weak comparable store sales trends and weak profitability. Kura Sushi's stock remains high, seemingly undermining the concerns over Kura Sushi's long-term growth story.

I initiated the stock at a Sell rating in my initial June 2024 article, titled "Kura Sushi's Growth Comes With Negative Caveats". Since Kura Sushi's stock has returned 7%, slightly below S&P 500's return of 14%.

Q3 Preview: Despite Expansion, Kura Sushi's Outlook Isn't Rosy

Kura Sushi is going to report the company's fiscal Q3 results from the March-May period on the 8th of July. Wall Street assumes Kura Sushi's growth to have continued with 13.8% revenue growth to a total of $71.8 million, but that profitability will stay thin with a slightly negative -$0.01 adjusted EPS. For comparison, Kura Sushi achieved a breakeven adjusted EPS in Q3'FY24.

Topline growth will continue to be aided by Kura Sushi's restaurant expansion - the company has added 14 new units in the past year, starting Q3 with a total of 73 units. Openings have continued at a rapid pace into the third quarter as well, and Kura Sushi guides for a similar pace of openings during FY2025 as the chain continues to expand throughout the US. When considering the fast pace of Kura Sushi's unit expansion, Wall Street's Q3 revenue assumption even suggests a slowdown in comparable restaurant sales, being a critical metric in the industry.

The expectation of slowing comparable restaurant sales may hold true - Kura Sushi's comparable restaurant sales declined very notably, by -5.3%, in the second quarter, underperforming most restaurant industry peers. While adverse weather understandably caused a traffic hiccup in February, seen by industry peers as well, Kura Sushi's traffic performance doesn't seem to be holding ground against competition. Despite an uncertain consumer, industry-wide sales trends have improved significantly for the third quarter period as is suggested by NRA's monthly survey results, but improvements in industry conditions may not be enough to push Kura Sushi's comparable restaurant sales growth positive. Kura Sushi's comparable restaurant sales seem likely to show a slight year-on-year decline in Q3.

In my opinion, the main focus point for Q3 and beyond is on Kura Sushi's profitability - the restaurant concept needs to drive healthy returns on investment for the aggressive unit growth strategy to be attractive. While Kura Sushi's operating cash flow is already positive at $18.0 million over the past twelve months, the company is still burning through cash to enable new restaurant investments. Underlying earnings remain thin, represented by thin GAAP operating losses. Added tariffs don't aid the earnings outlook as Kura Sushi has been noted to source the majority of its COGS from overseas.

As Wall Street's consensus for the Q3 adjusted EPS suggests, a profitability improvement doesn't seem likely for now. Keeping up healthy traffic is a critical ingredient in maintaining profitability in the industry, and Kura Sushi's weak comparable restaurant sales trend doesn't aid the earnings outlook. The second quarter showed a 230 basis point decline in the restaurant-level operating margin to 17.3%, related by the management to sales deleverage from weaker traffic. A growing amount of corporate expenses doesn't allow restaurant expansion to improve earnings very rapidly, and tariff pressure acts as a slight further headwind on earnings.

The long-term potential of Kura Sushi isn't ultimately weighed too much by a single quarter, but the weak immediate earnings prospects are still worrying. Kura Sushi had to raise equity in late 2024 to finance upcoming investments, already causing noteworthy dilution.

The company does now have a healthy balance sheet with no interest-bearing debt and $85.2 million in cash, enabling further growth investments over the mid-term without improved operational cash flow.

Kura Sushi's Valuation Remains Too Ambitious

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Kura Sushi.

I still estimate the company to continue its rapid expansion, creating a FY2024-FY2034 revenue CAGR of 12.0%. The larger restaurant footprint should ultimately bring operating leverage, and I assume the EBIT margin to reach a 9% level. From FY2024 to FY2034, the assumption suggests 14.2% of additional revenues to trickle into operating income growth.

The growth will still require Kura Sushi to invest extensively. I assume cash flow to stay negative over the next few years, enabled by Kura Sushi's strong cash, but that the company ultimately slows down expansion improving the cash flow conversion to a good level.

I again assume a 15 EV/EBIT exit multiple.

The estimates put Kura Sushi's fair value at $41.91, 53% below the stock price at the time of writing - even with good long-term growth expectations, the stock is valued at a significantly too steep price. When coupled with Kura Sushi's weak immediate earnings outlook, I believe that the stock provides a very weak risk-to-reward.

The fair value estimate is down from $45.20 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.75%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I still don't expect Kura Sushi to leverage debt in its financing. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.26% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran's most recent January estimates for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.44. With a liquidity premium of 0.25%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.75%.

Takeaway

Kura Sushi's Q3 will show a good amount of growth through expansion, but the underlying performance has quite a weak outlook as the company hasn't been able to maintain traffic. Despite a strong balance sheet, Kura Sushi's weak earnings also cause concern over the long-term growth story's attractiveness. With a valuation that disregards concerns and prices in very ambitious earnings growth, I believe that Kura Sushi's stock likely has weak returns ahead. I remain with a Sell rating.

data.title: Nu Holdings: Profitable Growth And Real Scalability In The Heart Of Latin America
data.description:
Since my last publication in April 2025, Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU) has continued to consolidate its place as a key player in the digital finance in Latin America. Its commitment to financial inclusion and innovation not only remains strong, but is expanding into new markets.

In Mexico, it already exceeds 10 million customers, driven by products such as "Nu Account" that simplify access to the financial system. In Colombia, adoption is also advancing, approaching 3 million users in a region historically marked by informality.

In technology, Nu reinforces its "AI-first" approach by integrating artificial intelligence to improve both user experience and operational efficiency. Since April, it has deepened its alliance with OpenAI, betting on more agile and intelligent solutions.

At a global level, the company is advancing its expansion with investments in digital banks outside of Latin America, especially in Africa and Asia. It's also considering moving its office to the UK: that could facilitate its growth in the US and other developed markets under more favourable regulatory frameworks.

In this dynamic fintech ecosystem, Nu is committed to digitizing access to credit, keeping pace with regulatory changes and maintaining the trust of millions of users.

While the current valuation is demanding-with a P/E TTM of 30.45x and a P/S TTM of 11.39x-, its sustained growth, healthy margins and solid cash generation support the thesis.

Therefore, I maintain a positive vision: NU remains an attractive buying opportunity for those who bet on the long term and the leadership of a fintech that is constantly reinventing itself.

A Solid Quarter With More Customers and Better Efficiency

In Q1 2025, NU added 4.3 million new customers, reaching 118.6 million users in total, with 98.7 million monthly assets. In Brazil, its main market, it reached 104.6 million users, representing 59% of the adult population. Activity remained strong with a stable ratio of 83%, reinforcing the quality of its user base and consolidating the model as a "primary bank".

Revenue grew 40% YoY in FX-neutral terms, reaching $3.24B, a new record for the company. Adjusted net income was $606MM, an increase of 37% YoY, while IFRS net income was $557MM, a growth of 74% FXN.

Despite higher spending on provisions and financial costs, NU managed to reduce its operational efficiency to only 24.7%-an improvement of 740 bps over the previous year-, consolidating its model as one of the most efficient in the financial world.

In addition, the ARPAC increased to $11.2 while the service cost fell to just $0.7, which still shows an impressive structural advantage of scalability.

Financial Strength, Limited Risk and Healthy Liquidity

The total credit and card portfolio grew 40% YoY FXN to $24.1B, while deposits increased 48% YoY FXN to $31.6B, achieving a healthy LDR (Loan-to-Deposit Ratio) of 44%.

While early default indicators (15–90 days) rose to 4.7% (+60 bps), loans with arrears >90 days fell to 6.5%, reflecting good risk management and performance above historical seasonality. As part of the company's conservative approach, provisions were made for nearly $974MM. This explains the compression of the risk-adjusted NIM, which fell to 8.2%, a move expected by management.

At the level of return on assets and capital, NU continues to excel: ROE of 28% and net margins of 38%, ranking in the highest percentile in the global financial sector.

A Demanding but Not Excessive Valuation

Currently, NU has a D+ valuation rating, showing that the market allocates relatively high multiples suggesting aggressive growth expectations but with uncertainty or high risk.

For example, the P/E TTM is placed at 30.45x, well above the financial sector (13.35x), implying a premium of 128%. The P/E FWD is projected at 25.29x, still quite high, although with a decrease expected as the company consolidates results.

With respect to value metrics such as P/S and P/B, NU exhibits multiples that far surpass the sector. The P/S (TTM) is at 11.39x, almost 2.75 times more than the sector (3.03x), and the P/B (TTM) is 7.50x, a multiple 5 times higher than the sectoral average.

This reflects that the market values its potential for growth and asset quality, but also indicates that the stock may seem expensive to more conservative or short-term investors.

Growth and Profitability: The Other Side of the Coin

Unlike valuation, growth indicators are outstanding. NU has an annualized revenue growth rate (YoY) of 32.91%, well above the sector (6.15%), and this trend is projected to continue with an estimated future growth of 31.85%.

Net income grows at an exceptional rate of almost 70% per annum and EPS also grows above 90% per annum (YoY), positioning the company as one of the fintechs with the highest acceleration in value generation.

This growth is reflected in profitability, with a net margin of 38.09%, more than double the sectoral average (22.47%), and an outstanding ROE of 27.91%, which triples the sector by far (10.22%). Its operating cash flow is also robust, with more than $4B in the last year, confirming that NU not only grows fast, but generates real cash, a key data to sustain investments and expansion.

Risks

Although NU has been growing strongly, its path is not free of obstacles. The rules in Latin America change often, and that requires it to adapt constantly without losing agility.

Competition is also tight: there are more new fintechs with fresh ideas, and traditional banks are no longer left behind. NU has to innovate all the time in order not to lose its place.

Digital security is another key front. Handling millions of sensitive data, any failure could damage the trust you worked so hard to build. In addition, it cannot ignore the economic and political context of the region. A sudden change can affect its users' pockets and, with that, the use of its services.

And finally, growing up outside is not just a matter of ambition: each country brings its own language, culture and rules, and adapting well to that takes time, effort and a cool head.

Conclusions

While I see the stock showing high multiples reflecting high market expectations, the reality is that it also has strong and sustained growth, with revenues growing above 30% YoY and net margins exceeding 38%. Its ROE is outstanding, close to 28%, and its operating cash generation is strong, demonstrating that NU not only grows fast but does so in a profitable and sustainable way.

In addition, NU does not stop innovating, betting on the AI that will allow you to optimize the customer experience and maintain competitive advantage. Its expansion in key markets such as Mexico and Colombia, together with its entry into new territories, augurs a future of significant growth.

In this context, I believe that buying NU is betting on a leading fintech in digital transformation, with the potential to generate significant value in the medium and long term.

data.title: EQT Corporation: Solid Growth Prospects And Balance Sheet To Justify Its Valuation
data.description:
In recent years, natural gas has gained more popularity amid the increased electricity consumption and the digital revolution. With more growth prospects on the horizon, EQT Corporation (NYSE:EQT) can be an ideal addition to our investment portfolio. Its decent topline performance and robust balance sheet promise sustainability in the long run. Technicals still have bullish signals, although potential retracements are expected. I am only concerned about its high valuation today compared to previous years.

EQT’s Recent Performance

EQT Corporation has taken advantage of the strong price and demand trend of natural gas in the past 12 months. After years of little change, electricity consumption in the US has risen again recently. These changes have been favorable for EQT’s production, giving it pricing flexibility and more incentives to produce. This also helped offset the weakness in its oil segment. As we can see, its natural gas sales volume reached 536,338 MMcf, a 7.4% YoY increase from 499,274. This notable increase happened despite the notable price increase in natural gas from $2.26 to $3.65 per thousand British Thermal Units or MMBtu. After all, it was still much cheaper than the prevailing price in the US during the quarter. Considering this, it’s not surprising that its operating revenue increased by 23.1% YoY from $1.41B to $1.4B. If we exclude the impact of derivatives, the revenue increase will be more pronounced at 85%.

It's also interesting to note that it managed costs and expenses very well despite the massive production spike YoY. Some of its expenses, like SG&A expenses, showed EQT’s enhanced efficiency as they increased proportionately with the operating revenue. It also helped that oil prices have weakened since the start of the year. This lowered transportation and production expenses, which mainly relied on fuel. As a result, its cost sensitivity measured by the operating leverage also decreased from 51.7% to 40.8%. This is a crucial aspect considering market volatility and stubborn inflation. Lastly, its operating margin soared from 13.0% to 28.6% YoY.

My Bullish View

Aside from its strong topline performance, I also consider its robust Balance Sheet as one of its core strengths. Despite having a high capital intensity, its cash position remains decent and adequate for its operations. This helps the company sustain its dividend payouts and buybacks. We can also note its strong FCF generation, which shows its high returns. In Q1 2025, it generated an FCF of $1.2B, which means that EQT converted about 50% of its natural gas and pipeline revenue into free cash.

It also maintains prudent debt management to ensure proper repayments. It combines its cash flow with refinancing strategies to remain liquid. As a result, it was able to reduce its debt by -40% from Q3 2024 to Q1 2025 while increasing its production level. It’s also good to know that 42% of its borrowings have maturity of at least five years, so it still has enough time to prepare for repayments. In addition, its Net Debt/EBITDA remains decent at 2.8x. This shows that EQT only needs about three years to pay all borrowings at once. It also proves that it has enough capital buffer and earnings to cover borrowings.

If we check the natural gas market as a whole, I believe EQT still has a lot of potential to sustain or even expand its production capacity. In fact, the US Energy Information Administration or EIA expects the natural gas price to settle at $3.80 per MMBTu. This is still 15 cents per MMBTu higher than the EQT natural gas price last Q1. For the succeeding quarters, EQT expects its natural gas production to be at 520-570 Bcfe. Suppose EQT produces just 520 Bcfe or 520,000 MMcf of natural gas, while its price is $3.80 with an uplift of only $0.10 to be conservative. Its natural gas revenue will be $2.02B, still higher than $1.96 in Q1 2025.

In my view, this positive view is not baseless. After all, EIA anticipates another spike in electricity consumption. It expects commercial, industrial, and residential segments to increase their electricity consumption per year until 2026 by 2.6%, 2.1%, and 0.7%, respectively. From 2020 to 2026, the total consumption will increase by 1.7% on average every year. If it reached 4,086 billion KWh in 2024, it will be 4,155B KWh in 2025 and 4,226B KWh in 2026.

For natural gas, 43% or 1,757B KWh of electricity consumption came from it. Using the estimated values, its share in 2025 and 2026 will remain high at 1,662B KWh and 1,648B KWh. While the numbers are decreasing, we can say that natural gas will not be totally replaced or eliminated, despite the calls to rely heavily on renewable energies. After all, this appears to be more reliable, consistent, and predictable considering the increasing electricity needs. Hence, its price increase to $4.20 per MMBTu is still realistic.

Another factor that will spur natural gas demand is the sustained expansion of data centers across the globe. This will continue as the digital and AI revolution intensifies. With more businesses and residential houses increasing their Internet usage, increasing the number and capacity of data centers is needed to ensure global communications and transactions continuity. Considering this, electricity consumption will increase further. In a recent study, the power demand of data centers may increase by 14 GW by 2030. This will require 2 billion cubic feet per day of natural gas power. By 2050, data center power demand is expected to increase by 153 GW.

Of course, we may wonder how long natural gas reserves in the Appalachian Basin will last, considering the fact that it supplies about 30% of the natural gas needs in the US. Thankfully, the EIA projects that natural gas production will still increase in the coming decades. Shipments will increase by 10-20 bcf/d by 2035.

How About Oil?

Oil remains a double-edged sword for EQT. The weak oil price trend leads to weaker oil revenues. Yet, EQT also enjoys lower transportation and production expenses to stabilize margins. In my view, its volatility and sensitivity to geopolitical factors remain a concern for the company. Currently, it’s $65.5 per barrel. And I think nothing much will change as long as the ceasefire continues and OPEC proceeds with its planned supply hike this July.

If the tension breaks out again, the price may break above $70 again. We must also consider the Strait of Hormuz blockade since it is crucial in the global oil supply chain. Voyage rerouting may disrupt the supply time and global market inventory. In the short run, it may become beneficial for EQT if buyers shift to suppliers outside the conflict region. These changes may also push the oil price up. However, this can be inflationary in the long run, not to mention the impending impact of new tariffs. Hence, oil price and demand in EQT may still be under pressure in the next twelve months.

Peer Valuation

Relative to its peers, EQT appears to be somewhat trading at a premium using EV/Revenue. Yet, I understand the optimism of the market. Its EV/Revenue of 7.58x means that the market expects its revenue growth to be the fastest in the coming years. I think that’s reasonable, considering its heavier reliance on natural gas than oil. Its growth prospects and better production and pricing stability add logic to this valuation.

For its profitability, EQT is slightly higher than the peer average. But it’s EXE that’s really trading at a premium at 26.75x. With respect to its book value, EQT is still reasonably valued relative to the group. HES trades at 3.7x its book value, making it the most expensive.

Technicals

EQT maintains some bullish signals as price momentum remains strong. As we can see, it is still setting new highs and lows above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs. Also, the 50-day SMA is still increasing above the 200-day SMA, showing that market sentiments are still positive in general.

I still advise some caution considering the bearish engulfing candle yesterday. This shows that sellers are trying to pull the price down after the market’s indecision last June 26, 27, and 30. On a lighter note, I think the trend remains logical if we look at its MACD and RSI. The stock has become oversold after the market’s strong reaction to the Israel-Iran tension. The price had to go down as buyers seek a favorable entry point. In addition, MACD and RSI remain in line with the price trend, so we cannot detect any bearish divergence.

It’s also interesting to note how the price reacts to the Fibonacci Retracement. We can see how the price direction shifts every time it hits a retracement point. So, I think the price decrease is warranted as it returns to the 0.786 point at $54.31. To ensure a bullish continuation, the price must not land below it. If not, it should stay above $53.72. For a more specific trading strategy, we can use a 1:1 Risk-Reward Ratio. If you enter at the current price, stop loss at $49.53. This is already close to the 0.618 point and may suggest a reversal. Take profit at $61.93 since this is already above the 1.00 mark, which may lead to a consolidation or a decrease to 0.786.

Conclusion

EQT Corporation is a solid company driven by its natural gas production. This is supported by its robust Balance Sheet and prudent cost and expense management. Growth prospects in the natural gas market from electricity and data centers may bolster its performance in the long run. Technicals are still promising, with some bullish signals and reasonable retracements. I am giving this stock a buy rating.

data.title: Zoetis: Growth To Continue Thanks To Companion Animals
data.description:
Nearly a year ago, I reviewed Zoetis (NYSE:ZTS) and stated that the company was a “Buy.” My reasoning was that the company remained a leader in a niche industry and has continued to grow revenue despite some product safety concerns.

However, since that article, the stock has declined by roughly 8% while the S&P 500 increased by nearly 12%.

As a believer in the long-term thesis that Americans will continue to treat their pets as members of their family and care for them, Zoetis remains a compelling investment given their status as a market leader in this specific healthcare sector.

Let’s dive into the most recent quarter as I will discuss the company's recent financials as well as their future growth opportunities.

Companion Animal Products Will Drive Future Growth

Zoetis collects revenue from products associated with companion animals as well as livestock. As you can see from the table below from the company’s most recent 10Q filing, Dogs and Cats make up the largest source of revenue for the company:

As noted in the company’s most recent investor presentation, Zoetis sees their market opportunity growing, in large part due to companion animals:

I believe spending will likely increase because pet owners love their dogs and cats. They view them as members of the family. As noted from company research, 86% of pet owners would do "whatever it takes" if their pet needed extensive veterinary care. This same research noted that even with a 20% decrease in budget, pet owners wouldn’t spend less on their pets.

Furthermore, many high-income families have multiple pets, many of which were purchased or adopted during the pandemic, and so these aging pets will need more care in the years to come.

Specifically, as it relates to companion animals, the company sees growth in the following areas:

I think these are areas which will certainly continue to impact both cats and dogs, and according to Zoetis’s management team, there is still a long runway for most of their growth catalysts. Speaking about osteoarthritis (OA) pain specifically, on the company's Q1 2025 earnings call, it was noted roughly 27 million dogs in the United States have some OA pain, only 9 million are being treated, 1 million are on Zoetis's offering, Librela, with the other 8 million on NSAIDS so there is certainly a large opportunity to gain market share.

Zoetis’s CEO, Kristen Peck, went on to state, “...We’re really seeing this as a huge market opportunity of the future. We’ve talked for a while that we think the overall market opportunity for us in a way is between $2 billion and $3 billion and we’re investing, as you know in a long-acting, OA monoclonal antibody that as we mentioned earlier in the year, we are expecting approval for this year. This would be a long-acting three month injection. It was a new and different antibody than Librela with the unique binding site and we’re expecting the dosing range to be about 10 times lower than Librela. So, obviously, we have a lot of confidence in continuing to grow this market.”

This certainly seems like a large market opportunity and although new competitors are entering the market (which I’ll discuss in more depth below), I’d still place my bet on an established market leader with the assets and an experienced management team to generate future growth.

Financials

Before diving into the current period, I’d like to zoom out and look at Zoetis’s growth over the last several years. As you can see below graphic from Zoetis’s Investor Day presentation, the company has steadily grown operational revenue at a pace faster than the animal health market:

Furthermore, since 2015 that company has steadily grown revenues, gross profits and net income, which is testament to the strength of the organization:

In the latest quarter, Zoetis delivered sales of roughly $2.2 billion, which is an increase of 1% compared to the prior year quarter. U.S. sales for the quarter were $1.2 billion, an increase of 2% compared to Q1 2024. International sales were $1 billion for the quarter, which was flat compared to the prior year first quarter.

Zoetis’s companion animal portfolio was the main driver of revenue growth for the quarter and accounted for roughly $1.5 billion of the company’s total revenue for the quarter. Zoetis’s animal companion revenues saw 8% growth domestically and 4% growth internationally, while livestock revenues declined. As it relates to animal companion products, the company’s flea, tick, and heartworm product Simparica accounted for $367 million, key dermatology generated revenue of $387 million, and osteoarthritis (OA) pain products, Librela for dogs and Solensia for cats, generated revenues of $147 million for the quarter.

Management upped their revenue guidance stating for the full fiscal year 2025, revenue would be in the range of $9.425 to $9.575 billion (the previous range was $9.225 to $9.375 billion).

From a balance sheet perspective, Zoetis does have more long-term debt than I generally like to see, however the company still has enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities as you can see below:

Risks

I think the largest risk for Zoetis is new competition. On the company’s latest earnings call, Peck addressed this concern when asked specifically about new entrants to pet healthcare, “I think what’s really important to note in both dermatology and parasiticides is we were the first mover and I think that’s really important because especially if you look at parasiticides, being the first mover made a big difference. Really, we’ve just been growing our share over time as we talked about.”

Peck went on to say Zoetis has a 40% share in puppies and noted once a dog gets on a certain product it’s unlikely a change would occur. In the case of dermatology products, those products have a 90% satisfactory rating, so it seems unlikely happy clients would switch to another product.

Despite new competitors, one reason I believe Zoetis will continue to dominate market share is due to their economics of scale and notable global operations. When discussing the tariff impact on the business I think Peck summarized perfectly why Zoetis can migrate such headwinds whereas other less established businesses would likely incur more financial costs, “From our global footprint and diverse durable portfolio across species and geographies, to our omni-channel presence, matrix manufacturing network and talented field force, combined those dynamics ensure for operating from a position of strength as we navigate a fluid environment shaped by macroeconomic, regulatory and geopolitical uncertainty.”

To me, the company's largest global presence, coupled with their years of expertise, makes if much more difficult for a new entrant to quickly grab market share.

I would say safety is another key concern for the business. Last year (as I noted in my prior article) the Wall Street Journal hit Zoetis with accusations that their drugs were unsafe. Zoetis refuted these accusations, stating that their drugs were safe, yet they did add a warning label for their drug Librela. Should further accusations arise or should a drug contain a serious defect or side effect, surely this would impact Zoetis’s market share and ultimately the company’s bottom line.

Valuation

Zoetis has a valuation grade of an "F" at Seeking Alpha:

As Zoetis is a profitable and established business, I still believe price to earnings is a good valuation metric for this business. Looking at Zoetis from both a forward GAAP and Non-GAAP price to earnings ratio perspective, both metrics are well below the company's historic five-year average.

As you can see below, Zoetis's non-GAAP forward price to earnings ratio is considerably lowered compared to this point last year:

From another viewpoint, in using a reverse discounted cash flow model with a discounted rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 2% Zoetis would need assumed growth of roughly 13% to justify the current share price:

This growth rate is beyond what most analysts believe is likely, as the majority have Zoetis growing in the mid to high single digits over the next five years. From this valuation viewpoint, Zoetis may see overvalued.

However, Zoetis does have a growing dividend which many investors will appreciate, as you can see below:

Zoetis has been slowly increasing their dividend payout over the last five years while competitors Elanco Animal Health Inc. (ELAN) and IDEXX (IDXX) don't even pay a dividend as you can see below:

Also, both Seeking Alpha and Wall Street view Zoetis as a “Buy” with Wall Street giving the company an average price of $195.04.

Overall, I think Zoetis's current valuation is reasonable, as the company's P/E ratios have gone down and the company's dividend continues to grow. I'm a firm believer in legendary investor, Terry Smith's stance that you buy excellent companies at reasonable valuations. Zoetis is a high-quality company; thus, it will never trade at a bottom of the barrel price. Long-term investors should feel comfortable buying at these levels, knowing steady growth will occur in the years to come.

Conclusion

Zoetis faces risks from new competitors within this niche industry. Also, there is always a risk of safety-related issues. However, I believe Zoetis has the economies of scale to stay ahead of the competition and reap the rewards, especially in the growing companion animal market.

Some may view Zoetis as an overvalued stock, but I think it’s trading at reasonable levels, especially compared to its price to earnings historical five-year average. If investors zoom out, it’s easy to see this company has grown revenues, gross profits, and net income at a constant pace. Furthermore, the company has been steadily increasing their dividend payout as well.

For patient long-term investors, Zoetis is a stock to own if you believe in the long-term thesis that pet owners will continue to care for their dogs and cats.

Market Review

  • For the 3-month period ending March 31, 2025, the MSCI EAFE Index rose 6.9% (in USD). Performance was broad, with nine of eleven sectors posting gains. The best performing sectors were financials (up 15.2%), energy (up 15.2%), and utilities (up 12.5%). The worst performing sectors were information technology (down 2.8%), consumer discretionary (down 0.7%), and real estate (up 1.3%). The five largest countries within the benchmark all rose during the quarter, with Japan up 0.3%, United Kingdom up 9.7%, France up 10.3%, Switzerland up 11.4%, and Germany up 15.5%.
  • Regionally, Europe delivered the strongest returns. Stocks were buoyed by investor enthusiasm around the continent's pledge to increase defense spending and Germany's fiscal pivot from austerity to stimulus. These positives outweighed the negative overhang of looming tariffs, at least temporarily. The information technology sector performed the worst, pressured by worries around the secular artificial intelligence theme. Purportedly cheaper and more efficient large language models have been developed by Chinese companies such as DeepSeek and investors continue to question the ultimate return on investment of massive capital expenditures by the hyperscalers.

Portfolio Highlights

  • The NYLI Epoch International Choice Fund (the “Fund”) generated a return of 8.7% in Q1, beating the MSCI EAFE Index by 180 basis points. The relative outperformance was due to security selection, particularly within financials, industrials, consumer discretionary, and communication services. Negative security selection within health care and real estate, partially offset these positive results. Sector allocation was modestly negative, with no single sector standing out.
  • Stock selection by country contributed to relative results with notable strength in the United Kingdom, Netherlands, and Japan. The allocation by country was negative, primarily due to underweight positioning to Switzerland, Spain, and Italy.

What Helped:

    • The top three contributors to total return were European financial companies: Lloyds Banking Group (OTCPK:LLDTF)(LYG), BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQF)(OTCQX:BNPQY), and NN Group (OTCPK:NNGPF)(OTCPK:NNGRY). In general, European financials benefitted from improving investor sentiment, driven by geopolitical developments including announcements of increased government spending.
    • Lloyds Banking Group is a U.K.-focused retail bank. The company reported good financial results and a strong outlook for the year ahead. Shares also benefitted from the U.K. financial regulator intervening in a lawsuit over unfair loan pricing in the motor finance market. Lloyds has a strong balance sheet and offers an attractive shareholder yield, in the form of ordinary dividends and share buybacks.
    • BNP Paribas is France's leading financial services company. The company reported better than expected 2024 results and upgraded its expectations for return on equity over the medium-term. Like Lloyds, the company offers an attractive shareholder yield with a well above market dividend yield enhanced by share repurchases.
    • NN Group is an insurance company based in the Netherlands, offering both life and property & casualty policies. The company's full year results showed a much better than expected capital position at year-end, supporting an attractive dividend and share buyback program.

What Hurt:

    • The bottom three detractors to total return were Novo Nordisk (NVO)(OTCPK:NONOF), Rohto Pharmaceutical (OTCPK:RPHCF), and Vonovia (OTCPK:VNNVF)(OTCPK:VONOY).
    • Novo Nordisk is a global pharmaceutical company specializing in diabetes and obesity care. Shares have been under pressure as weekly prescription data suggests the company's GLP-1 drugs are under- performing competing products from Eli Lily. However, the data is clouded by the presence of compounding pharmacies, who are making copy-cat versions of Novo's drugs. Compounders have been ordered by the Food and Drug Administration ((FDA)) to stop by mid-April, which should provide greater clarity on the underlying prescription trends.
    • Rohto Pharmaceutical is a Japanese manufacturer of over-the-counter skincare products, eye care products, and internal medicines. Shares were weak as the company reported disappointing fiscal third quarter results that called into question its ability to meet full year guidance. Management insists year-to-date results have been impacted by one-off issues and the phasing of expenses. The company expects to meet full year expectations.
    • Vonovia is a residential real estate developer and operator in Germany. The German government's decision to relax spending, deficit, and debt rules led to a rise in German bund yields. Investors tend to view rising yields as negative for real estate asset valuation, pressuring shares of the company. We think this view overlooks the tightness of the real estate market, with high occupancy and renewal rates.

Outlook and Positioning

  • The escalating tariff war has injected a lot of uncertainty and volatility into financial markets. The ultimate impact on the real economy and corporate fundamentals is largely dependent on the duration and magnitude of tariffs globally. While impossible to predict with any degree of precision, we expect global economic growth will be slower and inflation higher while corporate profitability will likely be negatively impacted by lower demand and tariff-induced margin pressures.
  • We continue to believe in constructing a well-diversified portfolio with an emphasis on stock specific risk. We believe the companies we own are well positioned to weather this storm. Businesses with strong competitive advantages that generate free cash flow, are appropriately capitalized, and run by excellent management teams are well prepared to navigate a lower growth, higher inflation environment. As we assess the portfolio's exposure to tariff risks, we find we own names across the spectrum of sensitivity. This is by design because neither we, nor anyone else, knows how this is all going to play out. We don't want to make a big bet on the ultimate outcome given a wide range of possibilities that span from a quick resolution and risk-on market to a global recession and further downside pressure.
  • While things feel very uncomfortable and perhaps even scary to some, our experience suggests that this too will pass. The current market environment feels very much like the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) in 2008 and the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. Ultimately, both episodes proved to be attractive entry points and buying opportunities for patient, long-term investors. We are seeing such opportunities emerge once again. The current market drawdown is giving us the opportunity to add to names that we fundamentally like at attractive valuations.
  • It is during moments like this that our investment philosophy and process matter most. Our investment philosophy remains unchanged, with a focus on free cash flow and capital allocation. We remain committed to managing our clients' assets against this tumultuous backdrop with rigor, discipline, and the humility to accept that the future is unknowable, but our philosophy and process can help guide the way.

The opinions expressed are those of Epoch Investment Partners, Inc. as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass. data.title: EIPI: Pipeline To High Yields?
data.description:
As a market junkie, I'm always looking for new funds to review, to see what the best and brightest minds of finance are coming up with. The FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF (NYSEARCA:EIPI) recently caught my eye, as the fund boasts an attractive $0.125 / month distribution, which annualizes to 7.7% based on the current share price of ~$19.50 (Figure 1).

Is EIPI's distribution sustainable, and are the fund's fundamentals attractive?

In my opinion, EIPI's distribution is sustainable, as the fund has earned ~9.0% average annual returns since inception, which more than covers the monthly distributions.

However, EIPI's strategy of selling covered calls on ~40% of its portfolio limits the fund's upside capture, leading to the EIPI ETF underperforming passive energy infrastructure ETFs.

With distribution yields roughly equivalent to passive energy infrastructure ETFs, I don't see the long-term appeal of the EIPI ETF. I rate it a hold.

Fund Overview

The FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF ("EIPI") is an exchange-traded fund ("ETF") launched by First Trust that is sub-advised by Energy Income Partners ("EIP"). EIP is a boutique energy investment specialist that invests primarily in "well-managed, publicly traded energy companies that own natural monopolies or provide services under long-term contracts that deliver stable and growing earnings." EIP's investment focus is on companies that can deliver high total returns through a combination of yield, growth, and capital appreciation (Figure 2).

In addition to investing in a portfolio of energy equities, the EIPI fund also employs covered call strategies on a portion of the portfolio to enhance the overall income of the fund (Figure 3).

The EIPI ETF was formed from the May 2024 amalgamation of several First Trust closed-end funds ("CEFs"), including the First Trust Energy Income and Growth Fund, the First Trust MLP and Energy Income Fund, the First Trust New Opportunities MLP & Energy Fund, and the First Trust Energy Infrastructure Fund. As a result of the mergers, the EIPI ETF has assumed the performance history of the First Trust Energy Infrastructure Fund (the "Predecessor Fund"), which had an inception date of September 27, 2011.

The EIPI ETF has over $900 million in assets under management ("AUM"), and charges a relatively high 1.11% total expense ratio (Figure 4).

Portfolio Holdings

EIPI's portfolio currently has 113 holdings, with the top 10 holdings accounting for 38.6% of the portfolio (Figure 5). EIPI's top holdings are predominantly energy infrastructure companies like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and integrated energy companies like Exxon Mobil (XOM).

The fund has also over-written 43% of the portfolio with out-of-the-money ("OTM") calls, with roughly a month in expiration (Figure 6).

Fund Performance

Historically, the EIPI ETF has performed well, with a 3-year, 5-year, and since-inception NAV average annual returns of 10.6%, 16.9%, and 9.0%, respectively (Figure 7).

The EIPI's low 10-year average return (5.5%) appears to be a timing issue, as the beginning of the period coincided with the 2014-2016 oil supply glut, which saw WTI crude prices plunge to a low of ~$26 / barrel (Figure 8).

Distribution

Considering the EIPI ETF has been able to generate long-term average annual returns of ~9.0% and the fund's $0.125 / share monthly distribution (7.7% yield), I believe EIPI's distribution is sustainable.

One way we can check distribution sustainability is to look at a fund's long-term net asset value per share ("NAV/share") (Figure 9). While EIPI's NAV does show volatility associated with its energy-equities risk, it does not show the amortizing NAV and share price pattern that is typical of amortizing return of principal funds like the Eaton Vance Limited Duration Income Fund (NYSE:EVV), which pays more than it earns (Figure 9).

EIPI, AMLP, Or XLE?

While EIPI's returns have been sufficient to fund its distribution, another question we should ask is whether EIPI ETF is the best expression of the underlying strategy of owning high-yielding energy equities.

For example, investors interested in energy equities with yield can consider the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) or the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE), both of which charge significantly lower fees than the EIPI (Figure 11).

Furthermore, energy equities in general pay higher-than-average dividends, with the AMLP ETF paying a trailing 7.9% distribution yield and the XLE ETF paying a 3.4% distribution yield, compared to EIPI's trailing 8.8% distribution yield (EIPI paid $0.2295 / share in short-term capital gains distribution in December 2024, leading to a trailing distribution yield higher than its 7.7% forward yield) (Figure 12).

At the end of the day, the single best comparison tool we should use is total returns, which captures both distributions and capital gains. Comparing the total returns between the EIPI, AMLP, and XLE ETFs, we can see that on a 5-year basis, EIPI has lagged significantly relative to AMLP and XLE (Figure 13).

This is most likely due to EIPI's strategy of selling upside returns, which limits the upside capture in EIPI's portfolio, especially after significant drawdowns like the 2020 COVID-19 market plunge.

A long-term comparison shows a similar pattern of underperformance by the EIPI ETF and its predecessor funds (Figure 14).

Where EIPI may outperform is if the underlying energy stocks were rangebound, like in the past year. In this scenario, EIPI's strategy of selling covered call options may boost its returns relative to the passive buy-and-hold strategies of AMLP and XLE (Figure 15).

Conclusion

The FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF is an energy-equities-focused ETF that sells covered calls on a portion of its portfolio.

The EIPI ETF pays an attractive 7.7% distribution yield that appears to be sustainable, given the fund's long-term earnings power.

However, relative to vanilla passive ETFs that hold energy infrastructure assets or high-yielding energy equities, the EIPI ETF's long-term returns lag significantly due to its strategy trading away some portfolio upside for premium income.

With the uncapped AMLP ETF yielding 7.9% on a trailing basis, I don't see why investors should buy the EIPI ETF with a cap on ~40% of their portfolio returns for roughly the same distribution. I rate EIPI a hold.

Market Review

  • The first quarter of 2025 ushered in a new era of uncertainty and volatility. By late March, we experienced an abrupt shift in investor sentiment following aggressive trade policy announcements from the U.S. administration.
  • We started the year with a consensus view on strong U.S. growth and weak inflation, but that view has since shifted to weaker growth and higher inflation.
  • Although recession odds were initially limited coming into the year, at the time of this writing, most market forecasters increased their probabilities significantly following Trump’s tariff announcements on Liberation Day April 2nd.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) remained on hold with markets initially pricing in two cuts in 2025. Pricing extended to four cuts once the degree and magnitude of the planned tariffs were clarified.
  • Spreads across sectors generally widened in the quarter, led by underperformance from lower quality issuers; cyclicals lagged on expectations for slower growth.
  • The Treasury yield curve bull steepened as rates fell further along the front-end. Fed Funds and 2-year rates inverted, which is unsustainable, in our view.

Portfolio Highlights

  • During the reporting period, the NYLI MacKay Securitized Income ETF outperformed relative to its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Securitized Bond Index. Portfolios with exposure to high quality, defined cash flow securities such as collateralized mortgage obligations fared better relative to unsecured credit risk. Rate volatility neither contributed nor detracted given the Fund maintained a neutral duration posture.
  • We have not engaged in any material portfolio repositioning, but we have reduced marginal risk in sectors that have performed very well over the last couple of years. We believe spreads will continue to widen near- term, and this will eventually present compelling value for re-entry. Although we are not ready to add risk at these levels, we are evaluating securities that have been “thrown out with the bath water” while we continue to sell names most directly impacted by the new tariffs (e.g. consumer cyclicals).
  • We maintain that relatively high absolute yields in fixed income combined with lower Treasury rates should still provide a buffer against near-term spread widening.

Outlook and Positioning

  • March and early April brought a whirlwind of trade policy pronouncements from the Trump administration, leading at times to significant de-risking in financial markets. At one point, the administration had announced tariffs that, if fully implemented, would have increased the effective tariff rate on goods imports by roughly 30 percentage points, to a level not seen since the 1870s. At time of writing, however, President Trump has announced a 90-day pause in his reciprocal tariffs excluding those on China, to allow time for bilateral trade negotiations. While this latest news reduces near-term recession risks, tariffs are still very likely to depress growth and raise prices in the near term. Chinese imports now face a 125 percent tariff rate; countries that faced new tariffs on April 2 will avoid the worst outcomes but will still face a 10 percent tariff; and many Canadian and Mexican products will still be tariffed at 25 percent. Sector-specific tariffs also remain in effect, while new ones are set to be rolled out shortly. Thus despite the 90-day reprieve on the most extreme tariff outcomes, U.S. households and business will still face meaningfully higher costs on imported items. In addition, uncertainty over trade policy will remain elevated for the foreseeable future, weighing on optimism and dampening discretionary household spending as well as business hiring and capital expenditures.
  • Higher tariff rates and ongoing trade policy uncertainty raise complications for monetary policy. With growth likely to slow substantially from 2024’s strong pace, the Fed will nonetheless be hesitant to cut rates preemptively given that tariffs will drive inflation higher this year. Still, once weaker growth becomes apparent and the unemployment rate begins to rise, we see scope for moderate rate cuts provided that inflation expectations remain well-anchored. Policymakers can also argue that the weaker growth outlook implies less inflation pressure once tariff effects are incorporated into prices. Still, we would not anticipate an aggressive rate cutting cycle. Inflation risks will remain elevated, and fiscal policy looks sets to loosen by the third quarter, once Congress finalizes measures to increase defense and border spending, extends expiring provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, and introduces additional tax breaks. As such, cautiously bringing the policy rate down towards a neutral setting over the next 12-18 months seems the most likely course of action.

data.title: NYLI Winslow Large Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • In the first three months of the year, U.S. equities pulled back following strong gains in 2024. Volatility spiked after Chinese Artificial Intelligence (AI) research lab DeepSeek released its model that was purportedly faster and cheaper to produce than its competitors and a mix of economic concerns and policy developments weighed on investor sentiment. Additionally, investors were focused on pending tariff news. Performance of the ‘Magnificent 7’ (Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), NVIDIA (NVDA), Amazon.com (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Meta (META) and Tesla (TSLA)) lagged for the quarter as market participation broadened.
  • U.S. large cap growth stocks underperformed in the quarter, with the Russell 1000® Growth Index down nearly 10%. The pullback follows two exceptional years of returns for growth stocks and the period saw leadership reverse into international and value indexes. We continue to see structural long-term advantages in U.S. large cap growth equities and the relative valuation gap versus other equity classes has returned to normalized levels.

Portfolio Highlights

The NYLI Winslow Large Cap Growth Fund (the “Fund”) outperformed the benchmark for the quarter. The Communication Services, Industrials and Consumer Discretionary sectors were key drivers of relative performance, largely due to stock selection. The Information Technology sector was the largest detractor, primarily due to stock selection. Our lack of exposure to the Consumer Staples and Energy sectors also detracted.

Outperformance also reflected the waning influence that we had anticipated of the ‘Magnificent 7’. While the group does have some correlation, we believe it is becoming increasingly heterogeneous and are actively managing each position. In aggregate, our active weighting of these holdings contributed to relative performance in the quarter.

What Helped:

  • The Communication Services, Industrials and Consumer Discretionary sectors were key drivers of relative performance, largely due to stock selection.
  • The top relative contributor to performance for the quarter was global leader in audio streaming and media services Spotify Technology SA (SPOT). The company reported another strong quarter and issued guidance ahead of expectations. The management team has pivoted to focus on profitability, in addition to revenue growth, and the shift has led to higher operating margins and free cash flow generation.
  • Arthur J Gallagher & Co (AJG), a leading insurance broker for the middle market, announced solid quarterly results, and management reiterated its outlook for continued strong organic growth. The pending closing of its AssuredPartners acquisition is anticipated to secure mid-teens growth in the coming years.

What Hurt:

  • The Information Technology sector was the largest detractor, primarily due to stock selection. Our lack of exposure to the Consumer Staples and Energy sectors also detracted.
  • The top relative detractor was AppLovin Corp (APP), a mobile technology company that provides software and AI solutions to help businesses connect with their customers. The company reported strong results during the period and released guidance that appears to be conservative. In addition, the company announced the planned sale of its gaming business which would position the company as a pure-play advertising platform with a higher margin structure as it expands into other verticals. However, the stock detracted from relative performance as several short-seller reports, which management claims are misinformed, were released and higher growth companies faced sentiment headwinds in the period.
  • Electronic payments provider Visa Inc (V) detracted from performance due to our underweight position in the stock; however, the portfolio’s aggregated exposure to fintech led to positive relative performance. Visa continues to benefit from the secular shift to digital payments and a reduction in regulations under the new administration could be an additional catalyst.

Outlook and Positioning

  • The typical New Year enthusiasm fizzled out fast this year for three reasons. First, advances in AI by DeepSeek and cautious commentary from Microsoft spooked the AI-levered stocks. Second, the scale of tariffs may be larger than previously estimated causing a negative short-term rebalancing in the U.S. economy. Third, forced de-risking by multi-strategy hedge funds added to the downside momentum. In a rare occurrence, U.S. indexes underperformed with the S&P 500® down 4.27% and the Russell 1000® Growth down 9.97%. The portfolio outperformed the Russell 1000® Growth Index as the largest weights in the Index underperformed. It was a good relative quarter for select active managers.
  • After two blockbuster performance years for U.S. large cap growth equities in 2023 and 2024, the correction in early 2025 is not surprising and has restored value to, we believe, the most attractive secular asset class in the world: U.S. Large Cap Growth. With an uncertain geopolitical backdrop, prices could continue to correct but politics didn’t create AI or the other major innovations that are driving profits. In other words, politics are transitory, and businesses will adjust and continue to innovate and drive profitability.
  • Consistent with the search for AI and innovation beneficiaries, the Information Technology sector remains the largest absolute weight; it is also the largest relative underweight. The largest relative overweight is in the Financials sector where the Fund continues to be overweight secular growers that could benefit from decreased regulation. The next largest overweights are the Healthcare and Consumer Discretionary sectors as select holdings are focused on the innovation and profit productivity themes. The Fund is underweight the Consumer Staples sector as the growth profiles and valuations of these companies do not meet the Fund’s investment criteria. The remaining sectors are within 1% of the benchmark and the Fund maintains no exposure to Energy, Real Estate and Utilities sectors.
  • From a Growth Type perspective, the Dynamic Growth sector is the largest overweight at 34% versus 30% in the benchmark. Consistent Growth is also overweight at 32% versus 31%. Cyclical Growth at 34% versus 40% remains the largest underweight.
